title: South Carolina Baptist Convention Passes Resolution Calling For Abolition
author: James Silberman
orientation: James Silberman reports on the South Carolina Baptist Convention's November 2021 passage of a resolution calling for the total and immediate abolition of abortion, while critiquing the Resolutions Committee for weakening the original abolitionist language and leaving the door open to pro-life incrementalism.
audiences: Movement historians tracking abolitionist progress in denominations

questions.question: Which prior denominational resolutions did the South Carolina Baptist Convention follow when passing its abolition resolution?
questions.audience: Movement historians tracking abolitionist progress in denominations
questions.answer: The SCBC followed the national Southern Baptist Convention, the Arkansas Baptists, and the Utah-Idaho Baptists in passing a resolution calling for the total and immediate abolition of abortion.
questions.quote: Following in the footsteps of the [national Southern Baptist Convention](https://freethestates.org/2021/06/abolitionists-pass-resolution-make-huge-strides-at-sbc-convention/index.html), [Arkansas Baptists](https://freethestates.org/2021/10/arkansas-baptists-pass-resolution-on-abolishing-abortion-at-state-convention/index.html), and [Utah-Idaho Baptists](https://freethestates.org/2021/10/utah-idaho-baptist-convention-calls-ut-id-governors-legislature-to-abolish-abortion/index.html), the South Carolina Baptist Convention passed a resolution calling for the total and immediate abolition of abortion on Tuesday.

questions.question: How did the South Carolina Resolutions Committee modify the original abolitionist resolution presented by the pastors?
questions.answer: While affirming the call for abortion's immediate abolition and equal justice for preborn children, the committee weakened the language by adding appreciation for pro-life defenders who have used their influence to restrict abortions through incremental measures.
questions.quote: While the committee affirmed certain important aspects of the resolution such as the need for the Church to demand abortion’s immediate abolition and equal justice for preborn children, they added that they appreciate “the passionate dedication of pro-life defenders who have used their influence to restrict abortions.”

questions.question: What did the committee say about the effectiveness of past pro-life incremental efforts?
questions.answer: The committee acknowledged that incremental efforts have not led to the end of abortion and concluded that the focus must shift to supporting laws that will actually end abortion.
questions.quote: they “acknowledge that these efforts have not led to the end of abortion, so we must now primarily focus our attention, concentrate our efforts, and bring our influence to bear in supporting laws that will end abortion.”

questions.question: What did the committee's added article say about repenting of incrementalism?
questions.answer: The article expresses repentance for being too quick to substitute pragmatic methods of protecting life when the focus should remain on the ultimate goal of ending abortion, while still supporting good faith efforts to restrict abortion.
questions.quote: While we continue to support all good faith efforts to restrict and reduce abortion, acknowledging the saving of any preborn life is a step toward saving all preborn life, we also acknowledge that our most fervent efforts must be focused on abolishing abortion once and for all, and we repent of sometimes being too quick to substitute pragmatic methods of protecting life when we should always be working toward the ultimate goal of ending abortion.

questions.question: How does Silberman characterize the committee's underlying view of pro-life incrementalism?
questions.answer: Silberman argues that the committee believes in moving past incremental substitutes for abolition and even repenting of the distraction, but does not view pro-life incrementalism as intrinsically wrong.
questions.quote: Essentially, the committee believes in moving on from pro-life incrementalism and even in repenting of being distracted with incremental substitutes for abolition, but doesn’t believe there is anything intrinsically wrong with pro-life incrementalism.

questions.question: Who met with the Resolutions Committee in advance, and what materials did they share?
questions.answer: Pastors Kiley Waldrop and Brad Starnes, along with SC abolitionist organizer Matt Brock, met with the committee a few months earlier and supplied copies of the Are You An Abolitionist pamphlet.
questions.quote: Pastors Kiley Waldrop, Brad Starnes, and SC abolitionist organizer Matt Brock met with the committee a few months ago to discuss the resolution and give them copies of our [Are You An Abolitionist pamphlet](https://store.abolitionistsrising.com/products/are-you-an-abolitionist).

questions.question: What two amendments were offered from the floor, and how were they received?
questions.answer: Waldorf moved to tighten the language regarding the life of the mother, and Starnes moved to add an exhortation calling abortion providers and participants to repent and believe the gospel; both amendments were accepted by the committee and the messengers before the resolution was passed.
questions.quote: Starnes then offered an amendment to include language exhorting “abortion providers and participants to repent and believe the gospel of Jesus Christ.” Like Waldorf’s, the amendment was received by both the committee and the messengers, before the full resolution was then passed.

questions.question: How does the resolution describe the duty of governing authorities at every level?
questions.answer: The resolution states that governing authorities at every level have a duty before God to uphold justice and to establish and enforce equal protection under the law for all born and preborn human beings.
questions.quote: (8) WHEREAS, Governing authorities at every level (federal, state, county, and municipal) have a duty before God to uphold justice asserting their God-ordained and constitutional authority to establish and enforce equal protection under the law for all born and preborn human beings (Romans 13:1-7; Micah 6:8; James 2:1-13)

questions.question: What position does the resolution take on exceptions in laws intended to abolish abortion?
questions.answer: The resolution unequivocally rejects any position that allows exceptions to laws intended to abolish abortion and to provide legal protection for all human life from the moment of conception.
questions.quote: we reject any position that allows exceptions to laws that are intended to abolish abortion and provide the legal protection for all human life from the moment of conception (Psalm 94:6; Proverbs 24:11; Psalm 82:1-4)

questions.question: How does the resolution treat ectopic pregnancy?
questions.answer: It distinguishes ectopic pregnancy from abortion, calling it a tragedy requiring medical triage in which doctors must continue to treat both mother and baby as human beings while exhausting all options to save lives.
questions.quote: (13) RESOLVED, That we recognize that an ectopic pregnancy is not an abortion, but a tragedy during which medical triage is necessary, with doctors never ceasing to treat both mother and baby as human beings, and upholding the Hippocratic Oath by exhausting all possible options to save as many human lives as they can

questions.question: Why does Silberman argue that the resolution, despite its weaknesses, still represents real progress?
questions.answer: Even though it leaves the door open to unjust incremental bills, it represents tremendous progress thanks to the work of South Carolina abolitionists and gives them leverage to demand officials support Jonathan Hill's bill to abolish abortion in South Carolina.
questions.quote: Even so, while not being an abolitionist victory, the resolution does represent tremendous progress that has been made thanks to the work of many South Carolina abolitionists who have had their hands to the plow for years. It also provides South Carolina abolitionists the ability to demand their governing officials support SC State Rep. Jonathan Hill’s bill to abolish abortion in South Carolina, lest they reject the position of both the Southern Baptist Convention and South Carolina Baptist Convention.

questions.question: How does Jonathan Hill characterize the convention's action and the role of the church in ending abortion?
questions.answer: Hill called it a first step with hopefully many more to come, and insisted that ending abortion is a political battle the church must lead, calling on the church to repent of its idolatry of political power and politicians.
questions.quote: “But the issue of ending abortion is a political battle that the church must lead. I am convinced that this is absolutely vital. It is my hope and prayer that this issue will be a catalyst for the church to repent of its idolatry of political power, politicians, and government institutions. The Gospel itself begins with a political demand to bow to the absolute authority of God over all. A Gospel that does not inform and direct our politics is not the true Gospel!”

questions.question: On what theological basis does the resolution declare every human being equally valuable?
questions.audience: Movement historians tracking abolitionist progress in denominations
questions.answer: The resolution grounds equal worth in the doctrine that all humans are God's image-bearers possessing equal, objective worth that does not vary based on ethnicity, age, means of conception, mental or physical development, gender, potential, or contribution to society.
questions.quote: (2) WHEREAS, As God’s image-bearers, all humans display divine worth, and possess equal, objective worth before God, that does not vary based on characteristics such as ethnicity, age, means of conception, mental development, physical development, gender, potential, or contribution to society (Romans 1:19-20; Genesis 1:27; 9:6; Matthew 18:6)

related.title: Abolitionists Pass Resolution*, Make Huge Strides at SBC Convention
related.reason: The original 2021 SBC resolution that the South Carolina Baptist Convention's resolution was modeled on and modified from.

related.title: Arkansas Baptists Pass Resolution on Abolishing Abortion at State Convention
related.reason: Explicitly cited in the article as one of the prior state conventions whose path the SCBC followed.

related.title: Utah/Idaho Baptist Convention Calls UT-ID Governors, Legislature to Abolish Abortion
related.reason: Also explicitly cited as a parallel state Baptist convention that passed an abolition resolution before South Carolina.

related.title: Bill to Abolish Abortion in South Carolina Filed by State Rep. Jonathan Hill
related.reason: The Jonathan Hill abolition bill the article urges SC abolitionists to demand officials support, with Hill himself quoted in this piece.

related.title: Point of Order! How Missouri Baptists Killed the Resolution on Abolishing Abortion.
related.reason: Companion piece showing the contrasting outcome of an abolition resolution at another state Baptist convention in the same season.

related.title: Oklahoma Baptists Pass Resolution Demanding an Immediate "End" to Abortion
related.reason: An earlier example of a state Baptist convention adopting abolition-style resolution language, providing precedent context.

related.title: Five Reasons Abolitionists Oppose Incremental Pro-Life Bills
related.reason: Provides the theological and strategic case against incrementalism that the article critiques the SCBC committee for not fully embracing.
